elements.sent-2411-001: Underwater imagery often suffers from severe degradation that results in low visual quality and object detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-002: This work aims to evaluate state-of-the-art image enhancement models, investigate their impact on underwater object detection, and explore their potential to improve detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we selected representative underwater image enhancement models covering major enhancement categories and applied them separately to two recent datasets: 1) the Real-World Underwater Object Detection Dataset (RUOD), and 2) the Challenging Underwater Plant Detection Dataset (CUPDD).
elements.sent-2411-004: Following this, we conducted qualitative and quantitative analyses on the enhanced images and developed a quality index (Q-index) to compare the quality distribution of the original and enhanced images.
elements.sent-2411-005: Subsequently, we compared the performance of several YOLO-NAS detection models that are separately trained and tested on the original and enhanced image sets.
elements.sent-2411-006: Then, we performed a correlation study to examine the relationship between enhancement metrics and detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-007: We also analyzed the inference results from the trained detectors presenting cases where enhancement increased the detection performance as well as cases where enhancement revealed missed objects by human annotators.
elements.sent-2411-008: This study suggests that although enhancement generally deteriorates the detection performance, it can still be harnessed in some cases for increased detection performance and more accurate human annotation.

elements.sent-2411-009: This is greatly attributed to the recent availability of large underwater datasets facilitated by the widespread use of {Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs).}
elements.sent-2411-010: Most recent datasets such as \cite{jiang2021underwater, liu2021dataset, liu2021new are fully or partially obtained by ROVs due to their relatively low cost, availability, and ease of use.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Because light propagates differently in water compared to air, underwater images are usually associated with limited visibility, reduced contrast, mono-color casts, and poor overall quality.
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, light underwater suffers from absorption by which the energy of the light is dissipated and scattering by which the direction of light is changed \cite{yuan2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Because of these challenges with underwater imagery, researchers have developed numerous enhancement approaches to mitigate these effects \cite{ACDC, zhang2023underwater, tang2022autoenhancer .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In addition, some studies \cite{li2016underwater, li2018benchmarking suggested using image enhancement as a preprocessing step to increase classification and detection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, very little previous work has addressed the use of Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) for high-level tasks such as Underwater Object Detection (UOD) and classification.
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, more work is required to investigate the effect of image enhancement on detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-017: Pei et al.~\cite{pei2019effects concluded that image degradation negatively impacts classification performance but image enhancement algorithms do not help regain classification performance.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The authors of \cite{fu2023rethinking, wang2023underwater concluded that image enhancement has a negative effect on detection performance when used as a preprocessing step.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Conversely, Chen et al.~\cite{chen2020reveal concluded that mixing enhanced and non-enhanced images to train a classifier does not lead to higher performance, but rather more robust performance.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Motivated by previous studies, we conducted a thorough evaluation of the underwater enhancement-detection framework and tried to overcome the shortcomings of previous studies by quantitatively and qualitatively linking image quality with detection, investigating the underlying reasons behind the degradation of detection performance after image enhancement, and showcasing how enhancement has the potential to improve detection.
elements.sent-2411-021: To support our study, we selected representative image enhancement models with innovative and very recent architectures based on the taxonomy presented in Section \ref{sec:related with focus on recent learning-based methods, then used two publicly available datasets to ensure the generalizability of our conclusions: CUPDD \cite{saleem2023multi and RUOD \cite{fu2023rethinking .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, the RUOD is one of the largest publicly available underwater datasets combining images from different datasets and containing different color casts, environments and aquatic creatures, while CUPDD is a relatively smaller dataset containing severely degraded challenging images.
elements.sent-2411-023: Since we do not have reference images (i.e. ground truth images with no water effects), we opt for a reference-free evaluation, similar to the work done in \cite{saleem2023non , using four reference-free metrics: 1) the Underwater Image Quality Measure (UIQM) \cite{panetta2015human , 2) the Underwater Color Image Quality Evaluation (UCIQE) \cite{yang2015underwater , 3) an underwater metric based on light absorption and scattering characteristics (CCF) \cite{wang2018imaging , and 4) the Matlab implementation of Entropy \cite{gonzalez2003digital .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: For detection, we opted for the state-of-the-art (SOTA) YOLO-NAS \cite{supergradients Single Shot Detector (SSD), which utilizes Neural Architecture Search strategy, and made a major advancement in the YOLO series.}
elements.sent-2411-025: YOLO-Nas has been successfully used in underwater environments outperforming other detectors from the YOLO family \cite{hamzaoui2024efficient, ercan2023gesture, awad2024underwater .}
elements.sent-2411-026: This work conducts extensive experiments and analyses to shed light on the effectiveness of various image enhancement techniques in improving object detection performance for underwater imagery leading to the contributions listed below:.
elements.sent-2411-027: The underlying reasons behind the deterioration of the detection performance in enhanced images is analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-028: A Quality Index (Q-index) is proposed that combines enhancement metrics to compare the quality distribution of the original and enhanced images.
elements.sent-2411-029: The relationship between image quality, as measured by enhancement metrics, and object detection performance, as measured by mAP is examined.
elements.sent-2411-030: A comparison is made between the cascaded approach of underwater image enhancement followed by object detection with that of detection performance with non-enhanced images.
elements.sent-2411-031: A qualitative and quantitative evaluation of SOTA image enhancement models on two datasets, namely RUOD and CUPDD, is performed.
elements.sent-2411-032: The remainder of this work is addressed as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: Section \ref{sec:related surveys enhancement-detection studies, state-of-the-art underwater enhancement models, detection models, enhancement metrics, and detection datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The adopted evaluation procedures and metrics are discussed in Section \ref{sec:eval .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Section \ref{sec:enhancement analyzes the obtained image enhancement results while object detection results are analyzed in Section \ref{sec:detection .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Then we analyze and discuss the effect of image enhancement on the detection performance in Section \ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, we conclude the paper with Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-038: We summarized and extended current underwater image enhancement taxonomies \cite{xu2023systematic, zhou2023underwater, hu2022overview comprising three main categories: non-physical, physical, and deep learning-based as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:taxonomy .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: These are further broken down into seven enhancement subcategories: histogram, fusion, retinex, dark channel prior, optical imaging, CNN-based, and GAN-based.
elements.sent-2411-040: In general, non-physical models work directly on images using image processing techniques without using any prior knowledge, so they are fast and simple but lead to sub-optimal enhancement details \cite{gu2023underwater .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Conversely, physical models rely on simulating the underwater imaging process \cite{jaffe1990computer and addressing ill-defined problems by using physical priors to mitigate the degradation process and obtain enhanced images \cite{liu2020real .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, the assumptions made for unknown parameters may not be entirely suitable or precise in intricate underwater environments, which restricts their use cases \cite{guo2017research .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Learning-based methods are based on neural networks and can generate good enhancement results but require parameter tuning and a sufficient amount of data \cite{liao2024underwater .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Since many recent enhancement algorithms are CNN-based, we select multiple models from this subcategory and insured that the selected models are very recent and well-designed.
elements.sent-2411-045: We also divide the combined task of enhancement and detection into two frameworks: 1) the cascaded framework \cite{fu2023rethinking in which detection is sequentially performed after enhancement, and 2) the joint-learning framework in which enhancement and detection are considered simultaneously \cite{liu2020real, fu2023rethinking .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The joint-learning framework is further divided into two subcategories: 1) shared loss \cite{fu2023rethinking in which the loss from the detector is used to train the enhancer enabling the enhancer to produce images that are favored by the detector rather than humans, and 2) reinforcement learning \cite{wang2023reinforcement in which image features are used as states, different UIE algorithms as actions, and loss reduction as rewards.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: It is worth mentioning that the joint-learning framework can only be implemented with learning-based enhancement algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-048: Therefore, we only consider the cascaded approach in this study.

elements.sent-2411-076: Underwater image enhancement is categorized into three main groups: non-physical, physical, and learning-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-077: The most recent models belonging to each category are summarized in this section, beginning with non-physical methods.
elements.sent-2411-078: Zhang et al.~\cite{ACDC introduced a method for enhancing underwater images using a combination of various techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Their proposed method included attenuated color channel correction (ACCC), a fusion-based contrast improvement method, and multiscale unsharp masking (MSUM) strategy to further sharpen the edges and details of the result.
elements.sent-2411-080: Extensive experiments were conducted on four benchmark datasets for underwater image enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-081: The results showed that their approach outperforms 12 other existing methods in both qualitative and quantitative metrics.
elements.sent-2411-082: Moreover, their method generalizes well to low-light images and hazy images.
elements.sent-2411-083: Nevertheless, there are some limitations associated with their method where the color of quality-degraded underwater images may be reduced after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-084: Besides, the introduction of a dual-histogram-based iterative threshold may increase computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-085: However, certain limitations were identified in their study.
elements.sent-2411-086: First, quantitative values may not always be the best.
elements.sent-2411-087: In certain special cases, images with higher evaluated scores may not necessarily have a visually pleasing appearance.
elements.sent-2411-088: Second, when dealing with rough images that contain a lot of unexpected noise caused by the camera, the noise may be amplified after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-089: Another algorithm, called Bayesian retinex, for enhancing a single underwater image with multi-order gradient priors of reflectance and illumination was proposed in \cite{zhuang2021bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The approach is based on combining a simple and effective color correction approach to remove color casts and recover naturalness.
elements.sent-2411-091: Then a maximum posterior formulation is utilized by imposing multi-order gradient priors on both reflectance and illumination.
elements.sent-2411-092: The comparative evaluation results demonstrated that Bayesian retinex outperforms other several traditional and state-of-the-art enhancement approaches on qualitative and quantitative assessments, and demonstrated the effective performance of the method in terms of color accuracy, challenging underwater scenes, an ablation study, parameter evaluation, and algorithm convergence.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Texture Enhancement Model based on Blurriness and Color Fusion (TEBCF) model is presented in \cite{yuan2021tebcf based on multi-scale fusion in which two inputs are merged; one to improve contrast based on dark channel prior in the RGB space and another to improve color based on the CIELAB color space \cite{chen2019towards .}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The authors used two datasets, UIEB \cite{li2019underwater and U45 \cite{li2019fusion , for evaluation with other baseline approaches.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Although the method introduced in \cite{yuan2021tebcf showed a significant quantitative performance advantage over other approaches, the enhanced images were not equally performing in terms of subjective analysis, which highlighted the weakness of the utilized enhancement evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Contrary to the non-physical methods described, physical methods incorporate some kind of prior knowledge based on the physics of the underwater image formation process.
elements.sent-2411-097: Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2023underwater developed an approach that begins with a color cast correction method for each color channel.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Subsequently, various enhancement methods are applied to enhance the base and detail layers of the V channel in the HSV space, which is decomposed using the spatial prior and texture prior.
elements.sent-2411-099: The researchers applied different enhancement strategies in different layers in order to enhance the contrast and texture detail of underwater images.
elements.sent-2411-100: Extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets were conducted, and the qualitative and quantitative comparisons showed that the proposed method outperforms 11 state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-101: Furthermore, their method showed good generalization capability for fog and low-light images.
elements.sent-2411-102: In another enhancement approach \cite{hou2023non , a new model called illumination channel sparsity prior (ICSP) was developed and a new dataset called Non-Uniform Illumination Dataset NUID was proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-103: The new model was based on the observation that there are some low-intensity pixels in the illumination (I) channel of an underwater image with uniform lighting in the HSI color space.
elements.sent-2411-104: Using this observation, the authors developed a variational model based on the retinex theory \cite{McCann2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-105: The authors claimed superior quantitative and qualitative results compared to other baseline approaches but our own evaluation in this paper reveals that the ICSP produces overexposed images, reducing some image details in the process.
elements.sent-2411-106: Next we turn to reviewing approaches based on deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-107: In addition to the aforementioned approaches, the field of underwater image processing has seen several other notable contributions.
elements.sent-2411-108: One such contribution is the Mean Teacher-based Semi-supervised Underwater Image Restoration (Semi-UIR) framework proposed in \cite{huang2023contrastive .}
elements.sent-2411-109: This framework focuses on incorporating unlabeled data into network training.
elements.sent-2411-110: Through extensive experiments, the results demonstrated the superior performance of their method over other SOTA algorithms, quantitatively and qualitatively.
elements.sent-2411-111: Fu et al.~\cite{fu2022unsupervised introduced an UnSupervised Underwater Image Restoration (USUIR) method by leveraging the homology property between a raw underwater image and a ``re-degraded'' image.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Their proposed idea decomposed the underwater image into three latent components.
elements.sent-2411-113: Then they generated a re-degraded image by randomly mixing up the estimated scene radiance and the raw underwater image, leveraging the homology constraint between the raw underwater image and the re-degraded image.
elements.sent-2411-114: Their experiments on two real-world underwater image datasets demonstrated the effectiveness and efficiency of their method in both inference time and restoration quality.
elements.sent-2411-115: And the success of the unsupervised framework highlighted the effectiveness of leveraging the homology property for unsupervised training through a suitable re-degradation operation.
elements.sent-2411-116: In \cite{wang2023domain , a Two-phase Underwater Domain Adaptation network (TUDA) was introduced, which simultaneously minimized the inter-domain and intra-domain gaps.}
elements.sent-2411-117: First, a triple-alignment network was designed to jointly perform image-level, feature-level, and output-level alignment using adversarial learning for better closing the inter-domain gap.
elements.sent-2411-118: Then, a simple efficient rank-based underwater image quality assessment method was performed, which can more accurately assess the perceptual quality of underwater images with the aid of rank information.
elements.sent-2411-119: Finally, coupled with the proposed RUIQA, an easy/hard adaptation technique was developed, which effectively reduced the intra-domain gap between easy and hard samples.
elements.sent-2411-120: Extensive experiments on four real underwater benchmarks demonstrated that their proposed method performed significantly better than other state-of-the-art algorithms, particularly in eliminating color deviation, increasing contrast, and avoiding over-enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-121: Tang et al.~\cite{tang2022autoenhancer applied NAS to address the challenge of processing severely degraded underwater images.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Their approach involved automatically designing a deep neural network architecture using NAS to search for the optimal U-Net architecture specifically tailored for underwater image enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-123: They proposed a new search space including diverse operators, applying the NAS mechanism to the transformer, and then proposed a selectable transformer structure that assigns the substantial learning capability to the proposed deep model.
elements.sent-2411-124: Their extensive experiments demonstrated that the proposed architecture could exploit the multi-color spaces in the underwater scenarios, that their framework obtained an optimal neural network, and achieved competitive performance on widely used benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-125: A different approach was explored in \cite{sun2022underwater , where underwater image enhancement was modeled as a Markov decision process (MDP).}
elements.sent-2411-126: This approach was based on reinforcement learning frameworks that selected a set of image enhancement actions and organized them into an optimal sequence.
elements.sent-2411-127: Experimental results validated the effectiveness of the reinforcement learning framework in underwater image enhancement, and the capability to easily integrate new image enhancement techniques was demonstrated, thereby enhancing the overall comprehensiveness of the framework.
elements.sent-2411-128: Object detection underwater is challenging due to a number of factors, such as the lack of massive underwater datasets compared to other vision-task datasets, and the severe degradation of the images caused by underwater effects.
elements.sent-2411-129: Nevertheless, researchers have developed a number of underwater two-stage and one-stage detectors.
elements.sent-2411-130: In general, one-stage detectors are faster compared to two-stage detectors but have lower performance.
elements.sent-2411-131: We will first talk about two-stage underwater detectors.
elements.sent-2411-132: Mandal et al.~\cite{mandal2018assessing integrated the Faster R-CNN \cite{ren2016faster with three distinct backbone networks to enable automatic detection and identification of various fish species in underwater footage.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Lin et al.~\cite{lin2020roimix introduced a data augmentation technique that uses candidate region fusion to create training samples that mimic overlap, occlusion, and blurring, thereby enhancing the model’s accuracy and robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Xu et al.~\cite{xu2021scale developed a scale-aware feature pyramid detector for identifying targets in underwater settings.}
elements.sent-2411-135: The approach presented in \cite{xu2021scale crafted a unique backbone sub-network to capture the fine-grained features of smaller targets.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Furthermore, a multi-scale feature pyramid was built to boost the feature representation for prediction by merging shallow-level texture details and high-level semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-137: This method successfully detected ocean targets but not in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-138: To effectively identify small underwater targets, Qi et al.~\cite{qi2022underwater introduced a two-stage underwater small target detection network with a deformable convolutional pyramid structure.}
elements.sent-2411-139: This was designed to tackle issues related to target deformation, occlusion, and scale variation.
elements.sent-2411-140: Other works have focused on one-stage underwater detectors.

elements.sent-2411-145: Chen et al.~\cite{chen2020underwater introduced a neural network architecture called SWIPENet, designed for the detection of small targets in underwater environments.}
elements.sent-2411-146: This network was built upon the framework presented in \cite{fu2017dssd and incorporates a sample weighted loss function.}
elements.sent-2411-147: The purpose of this function was to prioritize samples with higher weights while diminishing the focus on those with lower weights, thereby enhancing the detection performance for small-scale targets.

elements.sent-2411-152: YOLO-NAS \cite{supergradients is a significant upgrade over previous YOLO models, addressing several shortcomings.}
elements.sent-2411-153: The main features of YOLO-NAS are a new basic block that is quantization-friendly and a novel algorithmic optimization engine known as Automated Neural Architecture Construction (AutoNAC).

elements.sent-2411-155: Acquiring a substantial volume of underwater imagery can be challenging.
elements.sent-2411-156: Nevertheless, the availability of comprehensive underwater datasets is essential to the development of underwater image enhancement and object detection algorithms, especially for learning-based methods that need a large sample size for effective training.
elements.sent-2411-157: This has led to a concerted effort by researchers to compile comprehensive underwater image datasets.
elements.sent-2411-158: To support image enhancement efforts, many UIE datasets have been recently curated.
elements.sent-2411-159: For example, the Stereo Quantitative Underwater Image Dataset (SQUID) \cite{berman2020underwater contains images taken in different locations with varying water properties.}
elements.sent-2411-160: The Enhancing Underwater Visual Perception (EUVP) dataset \cite{islam2020fast contains separate sets of paired and unpaired image samples of both good and poor perceptual quality.}
elements.sent-2411-161: The Segmentation of Underwater IMagery (SUIM) dataset \cite{islam2020semantic contains over 1,500 images with pixel annotations for eight object categories.}
elements.sent-2411-162: The Underwater Image Enhancement Benchmark Dataset (UIEBD) \cite{li2019underwater includes 950 real-world underwater images, 890 of which have a corresponding reference image.}
elements.sent-2411-163: The Real-world Underwater Image Enhancement (RUIE) dataset \cite{liu2020real is divided into three subsets targeting three main challenges in underwater enhancement; those being quality, color casts, and task-driven.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Several datasets for object detection have also been recently proposed.
elements.sent-2411-165: The USOD10K dataset \cite{hong2023usod10k is a significant contribution to the field of underwater salient object detection.}
elements.sent-2411-166: It is the first large-scale dataset specifically designed for this purpose, containing 10,255 underwater images.
elements.sent-2411-167: These images cover 70 categories of salient objects across 12 different underwater scenes, providing a comprehensive range of environments and object types for researchers.
elements.sent-2411-168: The WildFish dataset \cite{zhuang2018wildfish includes an impressive array of 1,000 fish species across 54,459 images, facilitating the development of robust models for fish species classification.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Fish4Knowledge \cite{boom2012supporting is another widely utilized dataset, teeming with dynamic marine life such as fish, sea anemones, and various aquatic flora.}
elements.sent-2411-170: The Brackish dataset \cite{pedersen2019detection offers video footage of marine life like fish, crabs, and starfish in murky brackish waters, complete with annotations.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Datasets from the underwater robot picking contests, like UDD \cite{wang2020udd , DUO \cite{2021arXiv210605681L , and UODD \cite{jiang2021underwater , feature marine organisms such as sea urchins and scallops, compatible with popular dataset formats such as Pascal, VOC, and COCO, making them ideal for underwater detection tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: For broader underwater scenarios, Fu et al.~introduced RUOD dataset \cite{fu2023rethinking , which comprises 14,000 images spanning 10 marine categories, also capturing the intricate challenges of underwater environments such as haze, color distortion, and lighting issues.}
elements.sent-2411-173: The Challenging Underwater Plant Detection Dataset (CUPDD) presented in the work of Saleem et al.~\cite{saleem2023multi , consists of 414 images and categorizes aquatic plants into three broad types: bushy, leafy, and tapey.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Now that we have covered the breadth of algorithms and datasets used for underwater enhancement and object detection, we turn to our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-175: In this section we describe our experimental settings including the utilized datasets, metrics, and image enhancement and object detection models.
elements.sent-2411-176: We implemented our experiments on two underwater datasets: 1) RUOD \cite{fu2023rethinking , comprising 14,000 high-resolution underwater images, 9,800 of which are used for training, with approximately 75,000 annotations of 10 aquatic objects, such as divers, plants, and different types of aquatic animals; and 2) CUPDD introduced in \cite{saleem2023multi , comprising 414 images, 313 of which are used for training, with three general categories of aquatic plants collected in the Great Lakes region in the US.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: The use of both datasets help us to draw generalized conclusions since RUOD is very large, recent and extensive combining images from different smaller datasets and containing different color casts, environments and aquatic creatures while CUPDD is very challenging and has the potential of testing enhancement algorithms at the extreme end.
elements.sent-2411-178: To accurately represent underwater image enhancement, we select several SOTA image enhancement models covering major categories and subcategories of image enhancement \cite{xu2023systematic, zhou2023underwater, hu2022overview , including physical, non physical, and learning-based image enhancement categories.}
elements.sent-2411-179: We also insure that the selected models are very recent, well-designed and acquired from credible sources.
elements.sent-2411-180: Since learning-based algorithms became recently very common, we select multiple models from this category to better represent it.
elements.sent-2411-181: The selected models include ACDC \cite{ACDC , TEBCF \cite{yuan2021tebcf , BayesRet ~\cite{zhuang2021bayesian are non-physical methods that use histogram, fusion, and retinex-based enhancement, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: The physical methods are PCDE \cite{zhang2023underwater and ICSP \cite{hou2023non , which use a dark channel prior and optical imaging properties-based enhancement, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: The deep-learning methods are AutoEnh \cite{tang2022autoenhancer , which uses a vision transformer; Semi-UIR \cite{huang2023contrastive , which uses contrastive learning; USUIR \cite{fu2022unsupervised and TUDA \cite{wang2023domain , which are based on CNNs and GANs, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: The source codes and instructions to run these models can be found in a Github link within their corresponding provided references.
elements.sent-2411-185: We implemented all enhancement models without modification or additional training, using the source codes and the trained models provided by the original authors.
elements.sent-2411-186: On the detection side, the YOLO detection family has been extensively used in underwater scenarios showing its efficiency \cite{redmon2016you, zhang2021lightweight, liu2023underwater with YOLO-NAS being one of the recent top performers \cite{hamzaoui2024efficient, ercan2023gesture, awad2024underwater .}}
elements.sent-2411-187: In this study, the SOTA YOLO-NAS \cite{supergradients is fine-tuned with the original images and the enhanced images from each image enhancement algorithm for both datasets totaling of 20 model instances, each trained and evaluated separately.}
elements.sent-2411-188: The image resolution for both datasets is set to $800\times 600$.
elements.sent-2411-189: We used the SuperGradients \cite{supergradients library to implement the detector on a Linux server with two Nvidia Tesla V100 GPUs.}
elements.sent-2411-190: The training was performed with a batch size of 16 and the AdamW optimizer with a decay of 0.00001.
elements.sent-2411-191: We use the YOLO-NAS large (L) architecture and start from the COCO pre-trained weights.
elements.sent-2411-192: Researchers have developed a number of ways to quantitatively evaluate underwater images, which mainly fall into two categories: full-reference and reference-free evaluation \cite{verma2022systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-193: In full-reference evaluation, a ground truth reference image, resembling an image without water effects, is compared with the degraded image.
elements.sent-2411-194: In contrast, reference-free evaluation does not require a reference image, and instead is measured directly on the degraded image.
elements.sent-2411-195: In this study, we opted to use reference-free evaluation because reference images do not exist for the selected datasets \cite{saleem2023multi, fu2023rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-196: We also believe that this is a more practical study, as reference images are often impossible to obtain, except in very controlled laboratory experiments.
elements.sent-2411-197: We used four underwater image enhancement metrics:.
elements.sent-2411-198: UIQM \cite{panetta2015human , which is comprised of color, contrast, and sharpness indices.}
elements.sent-2411-199: A higher UIQM score indicates better image quality.
elements.sent-2411-200: UCIQE \cite{yang2015underwater , representing a linear combination of saturation, contrast and chroma.}
elements.sent-2411-201: A higher UCIQE score indicates better image quality.
elements.sent-2411-202: CCF \cite{wang2018imaging , which is a linear regression model of colorfulness, contrast, and fog density indices.}
elements.sent-2411-203: A higher CCF score indicates better image quality.
elements.sent-2411-204: Entropy, a statistical measure that can be used to describe the randomness of the texture in images \cite{Chen2019-kx .}
elements.sent-2411-205: Here we calculated it as the Discrete Entropy \cite{gonzalez2003digital using Matlab.}
elements.sent-2411-206: A higher Entropy score indicates richer details, i.e., higher image quality.
elements.sent-2411-207: All of the utilized metrics are used without any modification and are implemented with original weights and hyperparameter values from the original papers.
elements.sent-2411-208: We use the selected metrics described in Section.~\ref{metrics to generate a quality index (Q-Index) which is later used to describe and analyze the quality distribution of images.}
elements.sent-2411-209: The use of the Q-index would facilitate such analysis by making it more focused and providing a general impression about the quality of the images.
elements.sent-2411-210: Essentially, the Q-index is generated from the four selected metrics through outlier removal, global rescaling, and averaging processes to produce a single bounded metric between zero and one as introduced in Algorithm \ref{alg:cap .}
elements.sent-2411-211: A value is flagged as an outlier if it is three Median Absolute Deviations (MAD) away from the median as in the Matlab implementation in \cite{mathworksDetectReplace .}
elements.sent-2411-212: MAD is defined as \begin{equation}MAD = c \cdot median(\left| A - median(A) \right|), where  c = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \cdot erfcinv\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)},\nonumber\end{equation} and erfcinv is the Inverse Complementary Error Function \cite{mathworksInverseComplementary .}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}MAD = c \cdot median(\left| A - median(A) \right|), where  c = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \cdot erfcinv\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)},\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-213: Moreover, global re-scaling means that the minimum and maximum values are taken across all images from the original and enhanced datasets to fairly compare the Q-index values across different models.
elements.sent-2411-214: On the other hand, local Maxima and Minima refers to the Maxima and Minima of a certain metric belonging to a certain enhancement model as shown in Algorithm \ref{alg:cap.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Calculation of the Q-index}\label{alg:cap} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require A set of images from different enhancement models \Ensure Q-index values \State Calculate UIQM, UCIQE, CCF, Entropy for all images \State Remove outliers ($|value - Median| > 3 * MAD$) from all metrics \State Determine global Maxima and Minima across all models \For{model in models} \For {metric in metrics} \If{$(outlier - Median) > 3 * MAD$} \State Replace with local (per metric) Maxima \ElsIf {$(outlier - Median) < 3 * MAD$} \State Replace with local (per metric) Minima \EndIf \State Re-scale metric using global Maxima and Minima \EndFor \EndFor \State \Return $Q-index = Mean(re-scaled$ $metrics)$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-241: The change in the Q-index ($\Delta$ of Q-index) for an image is simply calculated as \begin{equation} \Delta \; of \; Q-index = Q-index_{(Enh)} - Q-index_{(Org)}, \end{equation} where $(Enh)$ refers to the enhanced image and $(Org)$ refers to the original.

elements.sent-2411-323: In this section, we provide a combined enhancement-detection analysis.
elements.sent-2411-324: In particular, we study the correlation between the enhancement metrics and the mAP.
elements.sent-2411-325: In addition, we conduct a further subjective analysis and provide cases where enhancement improves detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-326: To this end, we generate scatter plots for the mean enhancement metrics and the mean mAP for all selected models and for both datasets as shown in Fig.~\ref{corr .}
elements.sent-2411-327: Since the Q-index is made up of the four selected metrics, it will show similar results, hence, we opted for a more detailed analysis of individual metrics.
elements.sent-2411-328: The prevailing noticeable trend in Fig.~\ref{corr across all metrics is that the increase in enhancement metrics is not leading to an increase in the mAP.}
elements.sent-2411-329: In fact, enhancement metrics and the mAP have a slightly inverse relationship.
elements.sent-2411-330: For example, the mAP maintained similar values with the increase in the UIQM for CUPDD while slightly decreased by the increase of the UIQM in RUOD.
elements.sent-2411-331: Furthermore, some enhancement models achieved lower scores on some metrics compared to the original, yet performed similarly in terms of the mAP.
elements.sent-2411-332: For instance, the PCDE, ICSP, AutoEnh, TUDA, BayesRet and the Semi-UIR all performed worse than the original in terms of the CCF on RUOD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-333: The ICSP model is an outlier on most plots and gained lower enhancement and mAP values due to its extremely overexposed images.
elements.sent-2411-334: We, conclude that enhancement performance does not predict detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-335: This discrepancy can be attributed to two primary factors: 1) enhancement metrics are sensitive to noise and may not always align with human perception and 2) object detectors rely on different visual cues than humans to identify objects..

elements.sent-2411-347: Since most of the presented cases in Figs.~\ref{CUPDD_ENH_DET and \ref{RUOD_ENH_DET belong to low Q-index bins, we hypothesize that enhancement for low quality images could increase the detection performance compared to enhancement of the entire dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-348: We conclude that enhancement has the potential to increase detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-349: This improvement may be due to the fact that the enhancement process reduces the disparity between low and high-quality images, as discussed in Section \ref{q-dist.}
elements.sent-2411-350: i.e., By making low-quality, hard-to-detect testing scenes more similar to high-quality easy-to-detect training scenes within the same dataset, enhancement can facilitate better detection outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-351: This finding contradicts previous studies \cite{fu2023rethinking, chen2020reveal, pei2019effects, wang2023underwater which suggested that enhancement negatively impacts detection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-352: While such negative impacts may be observed when evaluating overall enhancement performance, our evaluation demonstrates that specific cases exist where enhancement can indeed boost detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-353: Therefore, we recommend a more detailed quantitative analysis to explore these effects further.

elements.sent-2411-369: In this work, we conducted a comprehensive investigation of underwater image enhancement methods and their effects on object detection.
elements.sent-2411-370: Our analysis included a quantitative and qualitative evaluation of different SOTA enhancement models, each representing a distinct enhancement category using two datasets, namely RUOD and CUPDD.
elements.sent-2411-371: A Quality index (Q-index) was proposed to assess the quality distribution of the images produced by these models.
elements.sent-2411-372: Subsequently, separate object detection models were trained and tested on enhanced and original images.
elements.sent-2411-373: A correlation study is also conducted between image quality and detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-374: Our findings reveal that enhancement significantly improves the quality of low-quality images but detrimentally impacts high-quality images.
elements.sent-2411-375: In addition, we found no significant relationship between enhancement metrics and detection scores.
elements.sent-2411-376: Although the detection model of the original non-enhanced images outperformed the detection models of the enhanced images, we revealed that enhanced images sometimes outperformed the original images.
elements.sent-2411-377: This enhancement effect is likely due to the reduction in the disparity between low and high-quality images, making challenging low-quality testing scenes more comparable to high-quality training scenes within the same dataset.
elements.sent-2411-378: Moreover, we provided cases where the detection models of the enhanced images detected cryptic objects that were missed in the ground truth annotations of the original images.
elements.sent-2411-379: Based on these findings, we recommend the following future directions: 1) enhancement should be used during the annotation process to help annotators identify hidden objects and thus generating more accurate datasets.

elements.sent-2411-408: Underwater imagery often suffers from severe degradation resulting in low visual quality and reduced object detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-409: This work aims to evaluate state-of-the-art image enhancement models, investigate their effects on underwater object detection, and explore their potential to improve detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-410: To this end, we apply nine recent underwater image enhancement models, covering physical, non-physical and learning-based categories, to two recent underwater image datasets.
elements.sent-2411-411: Following this, we conduct joint qualitative and quantitative analyses on the original and enhanced images, revealing the discrepancy between the two analyses, and analyzing changes in the quality distribution of the images after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-412: We then train three recent object detection models on the original datasets, selecting the best-performing detector for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-413: This detector is subsequently re-trained on the enhanced datasets to evaluate changes in detection performance, highlighting the adverse effect of enhancement on detection performance at the dataset level.
elements.sent-2411-414: Next, we perform a correlation study to examine the relationship between various enhancement metrics and the mean Average Precision (mAP).
elements.sent-2411-415: Finally, we conduct an image-level analysis that reveals images of improved detection performance after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-416: The findings of this study demonstrate the potential of image enhancement to improve detection performance and provide valuable insights for researchers to further explore the effects of enhancement on detection at the individual image level, rather than at the dataset level.
elements.sent-2411-417: This could enable the selective application of enhancement for improved detection.
elements.sent-2411-418: The data generated, code developed, and supplementary materials are publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/RSSL-MTU/Enhancement-Detection-Analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-419: Underwater computer vision has gained much attention in recent years with an increasing number of applications in areas such as ocean mapping, ecological monitoring, and inspection.
elements.sent-2411-420: This growth is largely attributed to the recent availability of large underwater datasets facilitated by the widespread use of Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs).
elements.sent-2411-421: Most recent datasets, such as \cite{jiang2021underwater, liu2021dataset, liu2021new , are fully or partially obtained by ROVs due to their relatively low cost, availability, and ease of use.}
elements.sent-2411-422: Underwater images are usually associated with limited visibility, reduced contrast, monochromatic casts, and low overall quality.
elements.sent-2411-423: This is attributed to two main phenomena: light absorption, by which the energy of the light is dissipated, and light scattering, by which the direction of light is changed \cite{yuan2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-424: Assuming that image enhancement has a positive impact on high-level tasks, some studies \cite{li2016underwater, li2018benchmarking suggested using image enhancement as a pre-processing step to increase classification and detection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-425: However, other researchers concluded that image enhancement, unintuitively, degrades the detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-426: For example, Pei et al.~\cite{pei2019effects concluded that image degradation negatively impacts classification performance but image enhancement algorithms do not help regain classification performance.}
elements.sent-2411-427: The authors of \cite{fu2023rethinking, wang2023underwater concluded that image enhancement has a negative effect on detection performance when used as a pre-processing step.}
elements.sent-2411-428: Conversely, Chen et al.~\cite{chen2020reveal concluded that mixing enhanced and non-enhanced images to train a classifier does not lead to higher performance, but rather to more robust performance.}
elements.sent-2411-429: Studies that address the use of Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) for high-level tasks such as Underwater Object Detection (UOD) and classification often have limitations.
elements.sent-2411-430: For example, previous studies analyzed the effect of enhancement on entire object detection datasets.
elements.sent-2411-431: In contrast, this study involves qualitative and quantitative analyses at the individual image level.
elements.sent-2411-432: This is done by visually inspecting hundreds of images and generating per-image mAP for original and enhanced images.
elements.sent-2411-433: As a result, we reveal that the conclusions previously drawn on entire datasets do not always hold true for individual images, i.e., some images perform better after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-434: This showcases the potential of enhancement in improving object detection.
elements.sent-2411-435: In addition, previous studies consider the quantitative and qualitative analyses separately.
elements.sent-2411-436: In this study, we generate figures to consider both types of analyses jointly, i.e., numerical values of quality are overlaid on the enhanced images and the detection results.
elements.sent-2411-437: Furthermore, we present a unique quality distribution analysis showing how enhancement degrades the quality of high-quality images.
elements.sent-2411-438: This work conducts extensive experiments and analyses using nine enhancement algorithms, three detection algorithms, two datasets, and four image quality metrics to highlight the role of underwater image enhancement in object detection.
elements.sent-2411-439: The contributions of this work are listed below:.
elements.sent-2411-440: We provide a joint quantitative and qualitative analysis, where images and their corresponding quality metric values are evaluated simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-441: This offers insights on the reliability of existing underwater image quality metrics.
elements.sent-2411-442: Furthermore, this analysis is further extended to include detection results, enabling a joint evaluation of enhancement and detection.
elements.sent-2411-443: We present a unique quality distribution analysis that illustrates how enhancement affects images of varying quality levels.
elements.sent-2411-444: We study the relationship between image quality metrics and the mean Average Precision (mAP), investigating whether image quality could predict the detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-445: We conduct a thorough image-level analysis instead of a dataset-level analysis, revealing the potential of image enhancement to improve object detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-446: The remainder of this work is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-447: Section \ref{sec:related surveys recent enhancement-detection studies, state-of-the-art (SOTA) underwater image enhancement models, and recent object detection models.}
elements.sent-2411-448: Details of the experimental setup are provided in Section \ref{sec:eval .}
elements.sent-2411-449: Section \ref{sec:enhancement analyzes the obtained image enhancement results, whereas Section \ref{sec:detection analyzes the obtained object detection results.}}
elements.sent-2411-450: Then we analyze and discuss the effect of image enhancement on detection performance in Section \ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-451: Finally, we conclude the paper in Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-452: In this section, we review combined enhancement-detection studies, Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) models, and Underwater Object Detection (UOD) models.
elements.sent-2411-453: A number of studies addressed the effect of image enhancement on object detection.
elements.sent-2411-454: For instance, the authors of \cite{chen2020reveal studied the effect of mixed domains and different environments on detection and discussed the enhancement role in the process.}
elements.sent-2411-455: The authors concluded that image restoration does not increase within-domain detection performance, but rather produces a more generalizable performance.
elements.sent-2411-456: One of the limitations of the study presented in \cite{chen2020reveal is that it is limited to a single restoration technique.}
elements.sent-2411-457: In a related effort, Pei et al.~\cite{pei2019effects examined the effect of synthetic image degradation and restoration on classification performance.}
elements.sent-2411-458: This study concluded that although image degradation severely affects the classification performance, image enhancement (i.e., degradation removal) does not lead to a restored or improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-459: This study can be extended to other higher-level tasks such as object detection.
elements.sent-2411-460: In another study, \cite{alawode2023improving introduced a new video dataset called Underwater Visual Object Tracking (UVOT) and an enhancement model designed to improve object tracking performance.}
elements.sent-2411-461: The study claimed significant detection performance improvement.
elements.sent-2411-462: However, no statistical significance analysis was provided in the study to validate the authors' claimed improvement.
elements.sent-2411-463: Another group of researchers \cite{wang2023underwater further questioned the effect of enhancement on detection.}
elements.sent-2411-464: In a recent development in image enhancement and object detection, Fu et al.~\cite{fu2023rethinking proposed a new method for combining image enhancement and object detection using a shared loss function to train both the enhancer and detector simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-465: The findings of their research indicate that the traditional enhancement approach of pre-processing the images with enhancement leads to a lower detection performance while a joint-learning approach increases it.
elements.sent-2411-466: The authors do not provide many details about the implementation of their proposed joint-learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-467: Moreover, the authors in \cite{wang2023reinforcement introduced a novel enhancement-detection joint-learning framework based on reinforcement learning (RL).}

elements.sent-2411-471: Underwater image enhancement is categorized into three main groups \cite{xu2023systematic, zhou2023underwater, hu2022overview : non-physical, physical, and learning-based methods.}
elements.sent-2411-472: In general, non-physical models work directly on images using image processing techniques without using any prior knowledge, so they are fast and simple but often lead to sub-optimal enhancement details \cite{gu2023underwater .}
elements.sent-2411-473: For instance, Zhang et al.~\cite{ACDC introduced a method for enhancing underwater images using a combination of various techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-474: Their proposed method included attenuated color channel correction (ACCC), a fusion-based contrast improvement method, and multiscale unsharp masking (MSUM) strategy.
elements.sent-2411-475: Although this method generalizes well to low-light images and hazy images, it may reduce the color quality of already low-quality images.
elements.sent-2411-476: This method is also computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-477: The retinex-based enhancement model in \cite{zhuang2021bayesian used multi-order gradient priors of reflectance and illumination.}
elements.sent-2411-478: This approach is based on a simple and effective color correction approach to remove color casts and recover naturalness.
elements.sent-2411-479: Then a maximum posterior formulation is used by imposing multi-order gradient priors on both reflectance and illumination.
elements.sent-2411-480: Another model called Texture Enhancement Model based on Blurriness and Color Fusion (TEBCF) is presented in \cite{yuan2021tebcf .}
elements.sent-2411-481: This model used multi-scale fusion to merge two inputs; one to improve contrast based on dark channel prior in the RGB space and another to improve color based on the CIELAB color space \cite{chen2019towards .}
elements.sent-2411-482: Although the method introduced in \cite{yuan2021tebcf showed a significant quantitative performance advantage over other approaches, it did not perform as good qualitatively.}
elements.sent-2411-483: In contrast to the non-physical methods described, physical methods incorporate some kind of prior knowledge based on the physics of the underwater image formation process.
elements.sent-2411-484: However, the assumptions made for unknown parameters may not be entirely suitable or precise in complex underwater environments, which restricts their use cases \cite{guo2017research .}
elements.sent-2411-485: For example, Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2023underwater developed an approach that begins with a color cast correction method for each color channel.}
elements.sent-2411-486: This method showed good generalization capability for fog and low-light images.
elements.sent-2411-487: In another enhancement approach \cite{hou2023non , a new model called Illumination Channel Sparsity Prior (ICSP) was developed based on the observation that there are some low-intensity pixels in the illumination (I) channel of an underwater image with uniform lighting in the HSI color space.}
elements.sent-2411-488: However, some images produced by this model are over-brightened.
elements.sent-2411-489: Unlike both physical and non-physical enhancement methods, learning-based enhancement methods rely on neural networks and can generate high-quality enhancement results but require parameter tuning and a sufficient amount of data \cite {liao2024underwater , which can be challenging for underwater environments.}
elements.sent-2411-490: Researchers in \cite{huang2023contrastive introduced a Mean Teacher-based Semi-supervised Underwater Image Restoration (Semi-UIR) model which incorporates unlabeled data into network training.}
elements.sent-2411-491: Similarly, Fu et al.~\cite{fu2022unsupervised introduced an UnSupervised Underwater Image Restoration (USUIR) method by leveraging the homology property between a raw underwater image and a 're-degraded' image.}
elements.sent-2411-492: This proposed approach decomposes the underwater image into three latent components.
elements.sent-2411-493: The design of this model allows it to run relatively fast and at the same time achieve good qualitative results.
elements.sent-2411-494: In \cite{wang2023domain , a Two-phase Underwater Domain Adaptation network (TUDA) was introduced, which simultaneously minimizes the inter-domain and intra-domain gaps.}
elements.sent-2411-495: First, a triple-alignment network was designed to jointly perform image-level, feature-level, and output-level alignment using adversarial learning to reduce the inter-domain gap.
elements.sent-2411-496: Then, an easy/hard adaptation technique was developed to reduce intra-domain gaps.
elements.sent-2411-497: The colors produced by this method are vibrant and the images are visually pleasing.
elements.sent-2411-498: Another group of researchers~\cite{tang2022autoenhancer applied Neural Architectural search (NAS) to search for the optimal U-Net architecture specifically tailored for underwater image enhancement.}
elements.sent-2411-499: This approach demonstrated good visual performance across different underwater scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-500: This is one of the preliminary studies on the use of reinforcement learning in underwater image enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-501: Object detection algorithms can be roughly categorized into one-stage and two-stage detectors.
elements.sent-2411-502: One-stage detectors generate object coordinates and labels in a single step, making them faster but generally less accurate.
elements.sent-2411-503: In contrast, two-stage detectors use a separate region proposal network to first identify potential object locations before classifying the objects, resulting in higher performance at the cost of speed.
elements.sent-2411-504: A group of researchers developed two-stage detectors for underwater scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-505: For example, Mandal et al.~\cite{mandal2018assessing integrated the Faster R-CNN \cite{ren2016faster with three distinct backbone networks to detect fish species.}}
elements.sent-2411-506: In another work, Lin et al.~\cite{lin2020roimix introduced a data augmentation technique that used candidate region fusion to create training samples that mimic overlap, occlusion, and blurring effects.}
elements.sent-2411-507: Xu et al.~\cite{xu2021scale developed a scale-aware feature pyramid detector based on a unique backbone sub-network which captures the fine-grained features of smaller targets.}
elements.sent-2411-508: Qi et al.~\cite{qi2022underwater introduced a detection model based on a deformable convolutional pyramid structure to identify small underwater objects.}
elements.sent-2411-509: Song et al. \cite{song2023boosting proposed a new model comprised of a region proposal network that provided the prior probability of objects, then the classification score and the prior uncertainty were combined to generate the score of the final prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-510: Finally, the classification loss is increased for miscalculated proposals, whereas it is reduced for accurately predicted proposals.

elements.sent-2411-516: Furthermore, the YOLO architecture has been used in several works.

elements.sent-2411-531: The RUOD is one of the largest publicly available underwater datasets, combining images from different datasets and containing different color casts, domains, environments, and aquatic creatures.
elements.sent-2411-532: It is comprised of 14,000 high-resolution underwater images, 9,800 of which are used for training, with approximately 75,000 total box annotations and 10 different classes.
elements.sent-2411-533: On the other hand, the CUPDD is a relatively smaller dataset containing severely degraded challenging images.
elements.sent-2411-534: It comprises 414 images, 313 of which are used for training, with three general categories of aquatic plants collected in the Great Lakes region in the US.
elements.sent-2411-535: The use of both datasets helps us draw generalized conclusions because the RUOD is very large, recent, and extensive.
elements.sent-2411-536: In contrast, CUPDD is very challenging and has the potential to test enhancement algorithms at the extreme end.
elements.sent-2411-537: We opt for a reference-free image quality evaluation because we do not have reference images (i.e., ground truth images with no water effects) using four metrics: 1) the Underwater Image Quality Measure (UIQM) \cite{panetta2015human , which comprises color, contrast, and sharpness indices.}
elements.sent-2411-538: All of the metrics used were applied without any modification and are implemented with original weights and hyperparameter values from the original papers.
elements.sent-2411-539: To facilitate and focus our image quality distribution analysis, we combine the four selected IQMs into a single quality index (Q-index).
elements.sent-2411-540: The Q-index is used throughout this work as a general indication of image quality and is used to describe and analyze the quality distribution of images before and after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-541: Essentially, the Q-index is generated from the four selected metrics through outlier removal, global re-scaling, and averaging processes to produce a single bounded metric between zero and one.
elements.sent-2411-542: A value is flagged as an outlier if it is three Median Absolute Deviations (MADs) away from the median based on the MATLAB implementation in \cite{mathworksDetectReplace .}

elements.sent-2411-563: The change in the Q-index ($\Delta$ Q-index) for an image is simply calculated as \begin{equation} \Delta \; \; Q-index = Q-index_{(Enh)} - Q-index_{(Org)}, \end{equation} where $(Enh)$ refers to the enhanced image and $(Org)$ refers to the original.
elements.sent-2411-564: This helps us understand how enhancement is changing the quality distribution of the images, i.e., whether enhancement uniformly improves the quality of images across the quality spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-565: On the one hand, by examining Fig.~\ref{fig:Q-Dist-CUPDD , it is observed that the entire original image dataset ranges from 0.25 to 0.75, with an almost bimodal distribution and a median of approximately 0.45.}
elements.sent-2411-566: One peak is centered around the median, and the other at just below 0.4, indicating that most images lie around those two peaks.

elements.sent-2411-568: The PCDE, with almost half the distribution in the negative, and ICSP, with the entire distribution in the negative, generally produce images that have lower quality than the originals.
elements.sent-2411-569: The TEBCF is a top-performer at a median of approximately 0.38, followed by the ACDC, BayesRet, Semi-UIR, USUIR, and TUDA at a median of approximately 0.2.

elements.sent-2411-571: It is also noted that for ACDC, TEBCF, BayesRet, Semi-UIR, and USUIR, the distribution has peaks at the top and tapering off towards the bottom indicating that these models are shifting low-quality images into high-quality images.

elements.sent-2411-606: Therefore, YOLO-NAS is selected as the best-performing detector and a total of 20 models are created from YOLO-NAS, including nine models for the enhanced versions of the CUPDD, nine models for the enhanced versions of the RUOD, and two models for the original images of the CUPDD and the RUOD.

elements.sent-2411-608: In this section, we show the results of the YOLO-NAS detector on the original and enhanced images of the CUPDD and RUOD.
elements.sent-2411-609: We call YOLO-NAS models that are trained on the Original images as original detectors, and YOLO-NAS models that are trained on enhanced images as domain detectors.
elements.sent-2411-610: On the one hand, the performance of the 10 YOLO-NAS original and domain detectors on the CUPDD dataset is shown in Table \ref{tab:CUPDD-DET .}
elements.sent-2411-611: The images of the CUPDD are severely degraded with very low visibility explaining the low overall performance of the original and the domain detectors, where the Original detector achieved the highest mAP by a notable margin at 0.38.
elements.sent-2411-612: The leading performance of the Original detector was evident in the Bushy class but not in the Leafy class where it outperformed by the AutoEnh domain detector by a notable margin of 3\%.
elements.sent-2411-613: This is possibly due to the fact that the bushy class has a more complex shape and edges that are obscured by enhancement noise compared to the simpler Leafy class.
elements.sent-2411-614: Unexpectedly, the PCDE achieves very close overall performance to the Original detector although we have discussed in section \ref{sec:enhancement how the PCDE produces very low-quality and non-visually pleasing images.}
elements.sent-2411-615: The ACDC and TEBCF showed lower Leafy class mAP at 0.22 and have overall scores of 0.34 and 0.33, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-616: BayesRet showed consistently lower performance across all classes with an overall mAP of 0.30.
elements.sent-2411-617: AutoEnh achieved the highest performance in the Leafy class at 0.31 and an overall score of 0.37.
elements.sent-2411-618: Semi-UIR provided consistent detection score across all classes with an overall mAP of 0.34, matched by TUDA.
elements.sent-2411-619: Finally, USUIR stood out in the Tapey class at an mAP of 0.40 and achieved an overall mAP of 0.35.
elements.sent-2411-620: On the other hand, Table \ref{tab:RUOD-DET shows the detection performance on RUOD dataset with similar results to Table \ref{tab:CUPDD-DET where the Original detector again outperformed all other domain detectors, apart from the AutoEnh which had a matching overall performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-621: Other models achieved moderate performances, including ACDC, TEBCF, BayesRet, and USUIR that showed reasonably consistent mAP scores across most classes, resulting in overall mAP values between 0.59 and 0.61.
elements.sent-2411-622: Conversely, ICSP consistently ranked with the lowest scores across nearly all classes, yielding the lowest overall mAP at 0.55.
elements.sent-2411-623: There is a noticeable trend where all detectors perform best in the Diver and Cuttlefish classes, often achieving mAP scores above 0.70.
elements.sent-2411-624: These observations signify the variability in the detection performance using different enhancement methods and across different classes.
elements.sent-2411-625: In addition, no domain detector was able to outperform the original detector indicating a negative overall effect of enhancement on detection at the dataset level.
elements.sent-2411-626: However, this general conclusion might not hold true on an individual level where enhancement could have a positive effect on some images and a negative effect on others, resulting in a negative overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-627: Therefore, a more granular analysis that addresses the detection performance before and after enhancement at the image level is required.

elements.sent-2411-628: On the one hand, the Original detector achieved top performance detecting most objects correctly as shown in Fig. \ref{det_vis_CUPDD , apart from the second and last images (counting from the left) where some objects were missed resulting in False Negatives (FNs) and other objects were confused resulting in False Positives (FPs).}
elements.sent-2411-629: On the contrary, most domain detectors increased the FPs and FNs.
elements.sent-2411-630: In addition, the True Positives (TPs) detected by the domain detectors generally have lower Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds compared with the Original detector which lowered the mAP$_{50-95}$ performance in section \ref{det:quant .}
elements.sent-2411-631: All domain detectors performed fairly well except for the PCDE and ICSP which have very foggy or overexposed images with little details and deformed objects' edges.
elements.sent-2411-632: We also observed that in the second image, the Original domain detector was not able to detect the Leafy plant but most domain detectors were able to detect at least parts of the Leafy plant.
elements.sent-2411-633: This indicates that the detection performance for this image increased after enhancement revealing the potential in enhancement improve the detection performance for some images.
elements.sent-2411-634: Furthermore, the Semi-UIR and the USUIR detected in the third image a leafy plant that is not in the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-635: We think that human annotators could not see the Leafy plant due to the severe degradation of the original image.
elements.sent-2411-636: Therefore, this detected object will be considered a FP despite the fact that it should be a TP.

elements.sent-2411-637: On the other hand, similar trends are found in Fig. \ref{det_vis_RUOD of the RUOD dataset where the Original detector outperformed domain detectors in most images.}
elements.sent-2411-638: Although some models did not produce visually pleasing images, they were still able to achieve good performance such as the ACDC.
elements.sent-2411-639: In contrast, some models that did produce visually pleasing images underperformed other domain detectors.
elements.sent-2411-640: Similar to our observation on the CUPDD, we observed some cases where domain detectors outperformed the the Original detector, such as the case for the third image.
elements.sent-2411-641: We also observe that domain detectors that produce more noise and artifacts have a relatively higher number of FPs compared to other models, such as the case of the TEBCF model.
elements.sent-2411-642: We conclude that the overall negative impact of enhancement could be attributed to diffused edges and increased noise after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-643: Furthermore, visually pleasing images do not necessarily lead to higher detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-644: In addition, image enhancement might improve the detection performance of some images and might also help in revealing hidden objects in the original images for human annotators.
elements.sent-2411-645: In addition, we conduct a further image-level analysis and quantify cases where the detection performance improved after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-646: In this section, we generate scatter plots of the mean values of the selected enhancement metrics for each enhanced dataset and the mAP of the domain detectors for both the CUPDD and RUOD as shown in Fig.~\ref{corr .}
elements.sent-2411-647: The prevailing noticeable trend in Fig.~\ref{corr across all metrics is that increases in enhancement metric values do not correlate with increases in mAP.}
elements.sent-2411-648: For example, the mAP maintained similar values with the increase in the UIQM for the CUPDD, whereas it slightly decreased by the increase of the UIQM in the RUOD.
elements.sent-2411-649: Furthermore, some enhancement models, such as the AutoEnh and PCDE, achieved lower CCF scores on the RUOD compared to the original images, yet their images performed similarly in terms of the mAP.
elements.sent-2411-650: In contrast, the TEBCF and the USUIR achieved higher CCF but lower mAP compared to the original images.
elements.sent-2411-651: The scatter plots presented in Fig.~\ref{corr do not show any consistent relationship between quality and detection metrics with mixed and random performances across different models.}
elements.sent-2411-652: This means that the enhancement performance does not reliably predict the detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-653: This discrepancy between the quality and detection metrics might be caused by the sensitivity of current quality metrics resulting in values that do not accurately reflect the true visual or structural quality of the images.
elements.sent-2411-654: Such outcomes may also be attributed to the fundamental differences between human and machine perception, further emphasizing the need for metrics that simultaneously account for visual quality from a human perspective and detection performance from a machine perspective.
elements.sent-2411-655: In this section, we illustrate how enhancement improves detection by conducting a comprehensive qualitative evaluation, examining hundreds of images before and after enhancement to assess its effect on detection.
elements.sent-2411-656: Based on the insights gained from this analysis, we then perform a per-image quantitative analysis to validate the observed effects and derive more insights.
elements.sent-2411-657: To further investigate the detection performance at the individual image level, we thoroughly surveyed individual images from both selected datasets and qualitatively compared their detection performance before and after enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-658: This time, we only look at cases where the domain detector performed better than the Original detector.
elements.sent-2411-659: This analysis is repeated for all domain detectors, and a representative image from each domain detector is selected to highlight the positive effect of enhancement on the detection performance as shown in Figs.~\ref{CUPDD_ENH_DET and \ref{RUOD_ENH_DET .}}
elements.sent-2411-660: To facilitate our comparison, the ground truth here is visualized using dotted boxes, whereas the detections are visualized using solid boxes.
elements.sent-2411-661: We first observed that the images presented in Figs.~\ref{CUPDD_ENH_DET and \ref{RUOD_ENH_DET are of low to medium quality, mostly achieving a Q-index below 0.5.}}
elements.sent-2411-662: Furthermore, as discussed in section \ref{q-dist that enhancement improves the quality of low-quality images; all enhancement algorithms in Figs.~\ref{CUPDD_ENH_DET and \ref{RUOD_ENH_DET increased the Q-index of the original images.}}}
elements.sent-2411-663: The lack of high-quality images in those cases, where the detection performance is better after enhancement, could indicate that enhancement of high-quality images results in lower detection performance compared to the original images.
elements.sent-2411-664: The enhancement and detection performances of different models varied in the CUPDD as shown in Fig. \ref{CUPDD_ENH_DET .}
elements.sent-2411-665: For example, the ACDC increased the contrast and removed the color cast of the Original image, resulting in an image that is not necessarily visually pleasing, but increased the detection performance with a much tighter box around the ground truth object.
elements.sent-2411-666: The TEBCF, BayesRet, and TUDA achieved a good enhancement performance with clearer objects' edges, turning some FPs into True Positives (TPs).
elements.sent-2411-667: Unexpectedly, the ICSP and PCDE also had cases where they enhanced the detection performance despite their overexposed or foggy images.
elements.sent-2411-668: The AutoEnh, Semi-UIR, and USUIR achieved similar performance and recognized undetected objects.
elements.sent-2411-669: On the other hand, similar trends can be seen in Fig.~\ref{RUOD_ENH_DET for RUOD dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-670: It is worth noting that some models enhance one aspect of the image and deteriorate others, but still achieve better detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-671: For instance, the TEBCF removed the color cast of the image but introduced too much noise in the process, but was able to detect the diver object in the image anyway.
elements.sent-2411-672: This could be explained by the diver class being one of the easiest and most distinct classes to detect in RUOD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-673: The TUDA produced a very visually pleasing image, making it easier for its domain detector to distinguish the turtle from the underlying rocks.
elements.sent-2411-674: This improvement may be due to the fact that the enhancement process reduces the disparity between low and high-quality images, as discussed in section \ref{q-dist .}
elements.sent-2411-675: i.e., By making low-quality, hard-to-detect testing scenes more similar to high-quality easy-to-detect training scenes within the same dataset, enhancement can result in better detection outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-676: These findings extend the conclusions made by previous studies \cite{fu2023rethinking, chen2020reveal, pei2019effects, wang2023underwater that enhancement has a negative effect on detection, which is true at the dataset level, but not always true at the image level, as our evaluation suggests.}
elements.sent-2411-677: Furthermore, most of the presented cases in Figs.~\ref{CUPDD_ENH_DET and \ref{RUOD_ENH_DET belong to low Q-index bins.}}
elements.sent-2411-678: This might indicate that exclusive enhancement for low-quality images could increase the detection performance compared to total enhancement of the entire dataset.

elements.sent-2411-679: Based on the observation that some images obtained higher detection performance after enhancement as discussed in Section \ref{Improving_Detection , we conduct a further quantitative image-level evaluation on the original and all generated enhanced sets.}
elements.sent-2411-680: In particular, the mAP is calculated for each original image and its corresponding enhanced versions.
elements.sent-2411-681: This allows us to compare the mAP performance across different variants of the same image.
elements.sent-2411-682: The variants associated with the highest mAP are selected to form a new mixed set, consisting of original and enhanced images produced by different enhancement algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-683: The per-image mAP values of the mixed set are then averaged to compute the overall mAP.
elements.sent-2411-684: Similarly, the per-image mAP values of all images in each individual set are averaged and presented in Table \ref{tab:mixed .}
elements.sent-2411-685: The mixed set achieves the highest possible mAP using the selected enhancement algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-686: Specifically, the mixed sets of CUPDD and RUOD achieved mAP scores of 0.64 and 0.77, reflecting improvements of 23\% and 9\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-687: The original sets recorded the second-best performance in both datasets, followed by different enhanced sets.
elements.sent-2411-688: Furthermore, we analyze the contribution of each enhancement algorithm to the mixed set by calculating the proportion of images sourced from each algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-689: This is done by counting the number of images each enhancement algorithm contributed to the mixed set and dividing it by the total number of images in the test sets of CUPDD and RUOD, as illustrated in Figs.~\ref{CUPDD_pie and \ref{RUOD_pie .}}
elements.sent-2411-690: Both pie charts reveal similar trends, with the original images comprising the largest portion of the mixed sets.
elements.sent-2411-691: The remaining enhancement algorithms show relatively comparable contributions, ranging from approximately 12\% to 4.12\%.
elements.sent-2411-692: This analysis highlights the potential of image enhancement to improve detection and support the development of further image-level evaluations aimed at selectively enhancing the images to boost detection performance without prior knowledge of their mAP performance.

elements.sent-2411-693: In this work, we conducted extensive experiments to evaluate recent underwater image enhancement methods and their effects on object detection.
elements.sent-2411-694: Our analysis included a joint quantitative and qualitative evaluation of different SOTA enhancement models on two datasets, namely RUOD and CUPDD.
elements.sent-2411-695: This joint analysis highlights a discrepancy between image quality metric values and human perception of quality, and reveals the limitations of current underwater metrics.
elements.sent-2411-696: In addition, a quality index (Q-index) was proposed to analyze the change in the quality distribution of the images after enhancement, revealing how enhancement improves the quality of low-quality images but over-enhances high-quality images, resulting in a negative quality effect.
elements.sent-2411-697: Moreover, we presented an evaluation of object detection performance on original and enhanced images, showing the negative effect of enhancement on detection at the dataset level.
elements.sent-2411-698: Furthermore, we conducted a correlation study between image quality and detection performance, showing no consistent relationship between image quality metrics and the mAP, and indicating that image quality cannot predict detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-699: Therefore, there is a pressing need to develop metrics that consider both image quality and detection at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-700: Finally, we conducted an image-level analysis instead of a dataset-level analysis to acquire conclusions at a granular level.
elements.sent-2411-701: In particular, we showcased images of improved detection performance after enhancement compared to the detection performance of the original images, indicating that enhancement has the potential to improve object detection.
elements.sent-2411-702: This is further validated by generating a top-performing mixed set containing original and enhanced images selected based on their mAP performance.
elements.sent-2411-703: The findings of this study provide insights for further investigation into the selective use of image enhancement on particular images to achieve improved overall detection performance.. \printcredits.
